channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Introducing ‘Project Freedom’
item.description: Trump’s latest gambit to reopen the Strait of Hormuz has inflamed the fragile cease-fire with Iran.

item.title: The EU wants to unshackle its economy. For real this time
item.description: Eurocrats are belatedly developing a healthy distaste for red tape

item.title: Narendra Modi has extended his grip on India
item.description: India’s ruling party scores a historic victory in West Bengal—but should beware voters’ unhappiness with incumbents

item.title: Bad government statistics can cost the economy billions
item.description: A new study tries to put a number on the value of reliable numbers

item.title: From Consensus to Consequence: Rethinking ASEAN’s Myanmar Approach
item.description: The self-admitted failure of ASEAN’s “Five-Point Consensus” demands an escalatory response.

item.title: Will Any Central Asian Leaders Head to Moscow for Victory Day 2026?
item.description: With the celebration expected to be toned down under possible threat of Ukrainian drone strikes, fewer foreign dignitaries are expected.

item.title: A Hungarian Playbook for Defeating Populists?
item.description: Advocates of liberal democracy everywhere are celebrating Péter Magyar’s triumph over Viktor Orbán. But among the lessons to draw from the Hungarian experience, the most important may be that toppling an illiberal populist is not as straightforward as much of the press coverage has made it seem.

item.title: U.S. War in Iran Leaves Ukraine’s Air Defense in Limbo
item.description: Ukraine and its partners are holding their breath to see what’s next for Patriot air defense missile deliveries.

item.title: Mali Is the Key to Understanding Africa’s Trajectory
item.description: The West ignores the warning signs in the Sahel at its own peril.

item.title: The Consensus on China’s Economy Is Strong—and Wrong
item.description: Calling on China to change its globally distortionary development strategy may well be warranted. But critics cannot question Chinese mercantilism on the grounds that it penalizes China's consumers, whose standard of living has risen faster than anywhere else in history.

item.title: Can Donald Trump reopen the Strait of Hormuz?
item.description: Probably not, and he still has to deal with Iran’s uranium

item.title: When Rumors of a Drinking Problem Sunk a Cabinet Nomination
item.description: The so-called Tower fiasco took place in a very different Washington to today.

item.title: What Orban’s 16-Year China Experiment Reveals About Europe
item.description: Orban amplified and politicized Hungary’s China ties, but the deeper drivers behind them extend well beyond one illiberal leader.

item.title: The Contradictions Shaping Japan’s Russia Policy
item.description: Will Japan’s Russia policy shift again – or settle into ambiguity?

item.title: China thinks America is declining but still uniquely dangerous
item.description: It sees Donald Trump as both symptom and accelerant of the decline

item.title: Uzbekistan Aims to Expand Extremism Law and Grow List of Extremist Crimes
item.description: In an attempt to further control religious expression, Uzbekistan has updated its blacklist of extremist sites and drafted a law to expand the list of related crimes.

item.title: In India, Medical Titles Debate Raises Public Health Concerns
item.description: A landmark case and recent judicial ruling highlight tensions between healthcare access, professional titles, and risks of quackery undermining public health.

item.title: What China’s AI Push Can Teach Africa About the Future of Labor
item.description: China’s 2026 Two Sessions suggest that AI and robotics are not simply eliminating jobs. They are reshaping labor demand unevenly.

item.title: Kim Jong Un Was Right – and Everyone Else Is Taking Notes
item.description: The war in Iran, alongside the 2022 invasion of Ukraine, has vindicated North Korea’s mantra: nuclear weapons are the only guarantee of sovereignty.

item.title: The Geopolitical Battle Over Monetary Infrastructure
item.description: The United States has raised the alarm about Brazil’s instant payment platform, Pix. That is because the public scheme has highlighted the infrastructural basis of monetary sovereignty, which now hinges not on exchange rates and reserve currencies but on who designs and governs the rails on which finance moves.

item.title: How a Political Odd Couple Finally Brought Japan and South Korea Together
item.description: Takakchi and Lee have the two U.S. allies on track to finally put their differences aside.

item.title: EU Delists Tajik Banks as Kyrgyzstan Feels the Heat
item.description: The removal of three Tajik banks from the EU sanctions list highlights diverging compliance trajectories across Central Asia.

item.title: Middle Powers Can’t Shape the Global Order
item.description: Trump’s war exposes the limits of collective action among the lesser powers.

item.title: Nepal’s Squatter Evictions Raise Due Process Concerns
item.description: A popularly elected government is being cheered even as it razes the homes of the most deprived and marginalized sections of Nepali society.

item.title: The End of America’s Soft Power
item.description: The United States has given up on one of its core international strengths.

item.title: The High Cost of Trump’s Crony Diplomacy
item.description: Effective diplomacy depends on credibility, consistency, and a clear alignment with national interests. The US administration’s personalized, opaque, and venal shadow diplomacy delivers none of that, and it will leave the US less respected, less trusted, and less effective on the world stage.

item.title: This Energy Crisis Is Undoing the Last Ones
item.description: The Western-led order that emerged after the 20th century’s three energy shocks is losing its grip.

item.title: Marcus Mietzner on Indonesia’s Decade of Jokowi
item.description: Joko Widodo was the country's first leader not to hail from the military or from an elite political family. What did his 10 years in office mean for Indonesia?

item.title: Arakan Army Chief Twan Mrat Naing on Talks With the Myanmar Government, and Relations With India and Bangladesh
item.description: “The military government wants to negotiate from a position of strength but we will not succumb to such pressure.”

item.title: Philippine Lawmaker Plants National Flag on Disputed South China Sea Sandbar
item.description: According to the activist Atin Ito coalition, the early morning visit to Sandy Cay succeeded despite a "heavy Chinese presence" in the area.

item.title: The End of the Axis of Abraham
item.description: The Arab Gulf and Israel have different visions for a new Middle East.

item.title: Why the Next India-Pakistan War Will Escalate
item.description: America will struggle to contain it.

item.title: Xi’s Forever Purge
item.description: The real goal behind China’s “self-revolution.”

item.title: Japan, Vietnam Agree to Bolster Cooperation in Energy, Critical Minerals
item.description: While in Hanoi, Prime Minister Takaichi Sanae also laid out her revamped vision for the "Free and Open Indo-Pacific" strategy.

item.title: Perceptions Matter – Whether Cambodians Like It or Not
item.description: Anger over an American media report referring to the country as “Scambodia” needs to be redirected to the root cause of an old pun.

item.title: The Myth of Vietnam’s Tilt Toward China
item.description: Analyzing Hanoi’s foreign policy from the perspective of its relationship with a single major power misses the forest for the trees.
